structured_choices.data_source: 10,000 labeled histopathology images collected from multiple medical institutions over 5 years. Raw images in standard formats (e.g., TIFF, PNG) at consistent resolution (≥20x magnification, ≥512×512 pixels). Associated metadata: cancer type (multi-class: adenocarcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma, small cell carcinoma, benign, etc.), stage (I–IV), patient age, institution identifier, slide preparation method, staining protocol, and scanning equipment type.
structured_choices.label_definition: Primary label: pathologist-confirmed cancer type and stage, obtained from institutional pathology reports. This is the ground truth for supervised learning. Secondary label: consensus of two independent pathologists for a stratified 10% subset to quantify inter-rater reliability and identify ambiguous cases. Any images with inter-rater disagreement are flagged and excluded from analysis to ensure label quality.
structured_choices.split_method: Stratified split by institution and cancer type to account for institutional bias and class imbalance. Method: (1) Group images by institution and cancer type/stage combination. (2) Within each stratum, randomly allocate 60% to training, 15% to validation, 25% to test, ensuring each set contains representative examples across all institutions and cancer types. This avoids both data leakage from institution-specific imaging artifacts and class imbalance. No temporal split is used because histopathology images do not have a natural temporal ordering within the dataset that would affect model generalization; stratification by institution and class is the appropriate control for confounding variation.
structured_choices.preprocessing_fit_point: All preprocessing transformations are fit on the training set only and applied identically to validation and test sets. Specific steps: (1) Fit image normalization parameters (mean and standard deviation per color channel) on training set. (2) Fit tile selection and focus detection (to identify tissue regions) on training images from all institutions. (3) Data augmentation (rotation, elastic deformation, color jitter within medical-appropriate bounds) is applied during training only; validation and test images are preprocessed but not augmented. This prevents data leakage and ensures the test set represents real-world deployment conditions.
structured_choices.features: Raw RGB pixel intensities from cropped tissue regions (1024×1024 pixels after focus detection and standardization). The CNN automatically learns hierarchical features (edge detection, texture patterns, cellular morphology, spatial arrangements). No hand-crafted features are extracted; this is the core distinction from the baseline. Pre-processing includes: (1) Stain normalization (Macenko method) to reduce institutional and protocol variation in H&E staining. (2) Focus detection to exclude out-of-focus or non-tissue regions. (3) Per-channel intensity normalization using training set statistics. These steps mitigate known confounds related to image quality and slide preparation variation without encoding diagnostic information.
structured_choices.model: Convolutional neural network (CNN) architecture: ResNet-50 pre-trained on ImageNet, with the final classification layer replaced to output probabilities for all cancer types and stages present in the dataset. Input: 1024×1024 RGB images. Output: softmax probabilities across K classes (e.g., 10 classes for cancer types/stages plus benign). Hyperparameters tuned on validation set: learning rate (1e-4 to 1e-2), weight decay (1e-5 to 1e-3), batch size (16 to 64), dropout (0.2 to 0.5), and training epochs (50 to 200). Early stopping based on validation loss.
structured_choices.baseline: Traditional feature extraction + logistic regression: (1) Hand-crafted features computed on training images: color histogram (64 bins per channel), local binary patterns (LBP, 10 radii), Haralick texture features (mean, variance, entropy from co-occurrence matrices), morphological features (nuclei density estimated via thresholding and watershed segmentation). (2) Features are z-score normalized using training set statistics. (3) A multiclass logistic regression classifier (with L2 regularization) is trained on the same training set with equivalent hyperparameter tuning via grid search on validation set (regularization strength C: 1e-3 to 1e3). (4) The baseline receives the same preprocessing (stain normalization, focus detection) as the CNN to ensure fair comparison. Both models are evaluated on identical train/val/test splits with the same preprocessing pipeline applied. The baseline represents the state-of-practice in histopathology analysis before deep learning.
structured_choices.primary_metric: Macro-averaged F1 score (F1_macro) computed on the test set. Justification: F1_macro is appropriate because (1) it balances precision and recall, both critical in cancer diagnosis—false negatives (missed cancers) and false positives (unnecessary treatment) are both costly; (2) macro-averaging ensures all cancer types are weighted equally, preventing high-frequency classes from dominating the metric and masking poor performance on rare but clinically important subtypes; (3) it is threshold-independent and thus robust to class imbalance. This aligns with the stakeholder priority to reduce false negatives while maintaining diagnostic accuracy across all cancer types.
secondary_metrics: Sensitivity (recall) per cancer type: ensures false negative rate is minimized for each clinically relevant subtype.
Specificity per cancer type: ensures false positives do not result in unnecessary interventions.
Macro-averaged precision: complements F1 to ensure the model is not merely guessing the most common class.

Calibration curve (expected calibration error): critical for clinical deployment—a poorly calibrated model may assign high confidence to incorrect predictions, leading clinicians to make poor decisions.
Confusion matrix per institution: identifies whether the model generalizes across institutional imaging variations.
structured_choices.model_selection_data: The validation set (15% of data, stratified by institution and cancer type) is used for hyperparameter tuning and model selection. Both the CNN and baseline undergo grid search or random search on this set. The final model is selected based on F1_macro on the validation set. The test set (25% of data) is held completely separate and touched only once at the end to compute final performance estimates.
structured_choices.test_set_policy: The test set is locked and not used during any stage of model development or hyperparameter tuning. After both the CNN and baseline models are finalized (trained on training set, tuned on validation set), they are evaluated on the test set in a single run. Test set performance is reported with 95% confidence intervals (via bootstrap) to quantify uncertainty. No re-training, re-tuning, or threshold adjustment is performed after test set evaluation.
structured_choices.confound_controls: Known confound 1 — Institutional imaging variation: Addressed via stratified split (institution is a grouping variable), ensuring all institutions appear in train/val/test. Stain normalization (Macenko) is applied pre-model to reduce protocol-specific variation. Confusion matrix is stratified by institution to verify generalization. Known confound 2 — Patient demographics (age, comorbidities): These are not used as features for the CNN (which operates on images alone), avoiding the model learning demographic shortcuts to diagnosis. A separate analysis compares model performance across age and comorbidity groups to detect if demographic bias is present despite exclusion from features. Known confound 3 — Cancer prevalence imbalance: Stratified split ensures all classes appear in all sets. F1_macro and per-class metrics are reported to prevent high-frequency classes from masking poor performance on rare subtypes. Known confound 4 — Image quality: Preprocessing (focus detection, stain normalization) mitigates this. Images with inadequate focus or extreme staining artifacts are flagged and excluded at preprocessing; the number of excluded images is reported per institution and overall.

design_narrative:
## Experiment Design: CNN vs. Traditional Feature Extraction for Histopathology Classification

### 1. Data Strategy

This study uses a dataset of 10,000 labeled histopathology images collected from multiple medical institutions over 5 years. Each image is labeled with cancer type and stage by board-certified pathologists. To ensure label quality, a stratified 10% subset (1,000 images) is independently reviewed by two pathologists; inter-rater agreement is computed, and any images with disagreement are flagged for exclusion to prevent label noise from inflating performance estimates.

Known data quality issues are addressed proactively: (1) stain variation across institutions is corrected using Macenko stain normalization, applied consistently pre-model; (2) out-of-focus and non-tissue regions are identified via automated focus detection and cropped; (3) images with inadequate quality metrics are excluded, and the number of exclusions per institution is documented to identify systematic problems.

### 2. Split Strategy

A stratified split is employed, grouping images by institution and cancer type/stage combination. Within each stratum, 60% of images are randomly assigned to the training set, 15% to the validation set, and 25% to the test set. This approach prevents institutional and class-imbalance confounds: all cancer types and all institutions appear in each set at representative frequencies, avoiding spurious model performance inflated by high-prevalence classes or institution-specific artifacts. No temporal split is used because images lack a temporal ordering that would predict future outcomes; stratification by institution and class is the appropriate control.

The split is finalized before any model training begins. Random seeds are fixed and documented to ensure reproducibility.

### 3. Feature Engineering

For the CNN model, raw RGB pixel intensities from 1024×1024 tissue regions serve as input. The model automatically learns hierarchical convolutional features. Pre-processing (stain normalization, focus detection, per-channel intensity normalization) is fit exclusively on the training set and applied identically to validation and test sets to prevent data leakage.

For the baseline (traditional feature extraction + logistic regression), hand-crafted features are computed on training images: color histograms (64 bins per channel), local binary patterns (LBP across multiple radii), Haralick texture statistics, and morphological features (nuclei density via watershed segmentation). These features are computed identically for all three sets but normalized (z-score) using training set statistics only.

### 4. Model and Baseline

The proposed model is a ResNet-50 CNN pre-trained on ImageNet, with the final classification layer adapted for the dataset's cancer types and stages. The baseline is a logistic regression classifier trained on hand-crafted features extracted via traditional image processing methods. Both models undergo equivalent hyperparameter tuning via grid search on the validation set: the CNN tuner optimizes learning rate, weight decay, batch size, dropout, and training epochs; the baseline tuner optimizes regularization strength (C) and feature subsets. Both receive identical preprocessing and are evaluated on the same splits. This ensures the comparison isolates the contribution of learned convolutional features versus hand-crafted features, with confounds held constant.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is macro-averaged F1 score (F1_macro) on the test set. This metric is chosen because (1) it balances precision and recall, both critical in oncology—false negatives risk patient harm via misdiagnosis; false positives risk unnecessary treatment; (2) macro-averaging prevents high-frequency cancer types from dominating the metric, ensuring the model performs adequately on rare but clinically important subtypes; (3) it is threshold-independent and thus appropriate for clinical deployment where decision thresholds can be adjusted post-hoc.

Secondary metrics include per-class sensitivity and specificity (to identify if any cancer type is systematically under- or over-detected), calibration metrics (to ensure predicted probabilities reflect true likelihoods, essential for clinician confidence), and institution-stratified confusion matrices (to verify generalization across imaging protocols).

### 6. Validation Approach

The validation set is used exclusively for hyperparameter tuning and early stopping. Models are trained on the training set and evaluated on the validation set; the model state yielding the best F1_macro on validation is selected. The test set is sealed and evaluated only once after model finalization, preventing overfitting to test data and ensuring an unbiased estimate of deployment performance.

### 7. Controls and Confounds

Institutional imaging variation is controlled via stratified splits and stain normalization. Patient demographics (age, comorbidities) are excluded from model inputs, preventing demographic shortcuts to diagnosis; however, a post-hoc subgroup analysis stratifies test performance by patient age and comorbidity groups to detect whether demographic bias emerges despite feature exclusion. Class imbalance is addressed via F1_macro and per-class metrics rather than accuracy, ensuring all cancer types contribute equally to the reported performance.

The experiment establishes whether CNN-based feature learning outperforms hand-crafted features when both are given equal preprocessing and tuning effort on this multi-institutional dataset. It does not establish whether the CNN generalizes to unseen institutions or imaging modalities outside the training data distribution—that would require external validation.

### 8. Final Test Evaluation

After both models are finalized, they are applied to the test set in a single run. F1_macro, per-class sensitivity/specificity, and calibration metrics are reported with 95% bootstrap confidence intervals. Confusion matrices are stratified by institution and cancer type. Results are compared via McNemar's test (for paired proportions) to determine whether the CNN significantly outperforms the baseline. All code, hyperparameters, and random seeds are documented for reproducibility.
